Example 254 | Profile: older_adults | Language: English | ID/Index: 254
Source Text
Bedside neurologic testing Neuropsychologic testing
Generated Simplification
### Understanding Neurologic and Neuropsychologic Testing #### What is Neurologic Testing? Neurologic testing is a way for doctors to check how well your brain, nerves, and muscles are working. This is often done at your bedside during a medical visit. The doctor may ask you to do simple tasks, like moving your arms or legs, following a light with your eyes, or answering questions. These tests help the doctor find out if there are any problems with your nervous system. #### What is Neuropsychologic Testing? Neuropsychologic testing is a more detailed way to check how your brain is working. It looks at things like memory, attention, problem-solving, and language skills. These tests are usually done by a specialist and take longer than bedside tests. They can help find problems that might not show up in a quick exam. #### Why Are These Tests Important? - Neurologic testing helps find problems with your nerves, muscles, or brain that might cause symptoms like weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking. - Neuropsychologic testing can help diagnose conditions like memory loss, dementia, or other brain-related issues. #### What Should You Do? - If your doctor recommends these tests, it’s important to follow their advice. These tests can help find the cause of your symptoms and guide your treatment. - If you have questions about what the tests involve or why they are needed, ask your doctor. They can explain how the tests will help in your situation. #### When to Talk to Your Doctor - If you notice changes in your memory, thinking, or movement, let your doctor know. They may suggest one of these tests. - If you are scheduled for testing, ask your doctor what to expect and how to prepare. These tests are tools to help your doctor understand your health better. Always discuss any concerns or questions with your healthcare provider.
Reference Simplification
A doctor 's evaluation Standardized tests of brain function

Precision
-0.365
Recall
0.101
F1
-0.141

Example 257 | Profile: older_adults | Language: English | ID/Index: 257
Source Text
Cardiac complications include pericarditis (most commonly) and myocarditis.
Generated Simplification
Heart-related problems can happen and may include: - **Pericarditis**: This is the most common issue. It means swelling or irritation of the thin sac around the heart. - **Myocarditis**: This is less common. It means swelling of the heart muscle itself. If you have concerns about these conditions or symptoms like chest pain, shortness of breath, or unusual fatigue, talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
People with lupus may have chest pain due to inflammation of the sac around the heart pericarditis). More serious but rare effects on the heart are inflammation of the walls of the coronary arteries (coronary artery vasculitis), which can lead to angina, and inflammation of the heart muscle (myocarditis), which can lead to heart failure.

Example 258 | Profile: older_adults | Language: English | ID/Index: 258
Source Text
Lifestyle changes have been shown to be effective in reducing (and in the case of diet, reversing) coronary disease: In people with coronary artery disease, aerobic exercise can reduce the risk of mortality.
Generated Simplification
Making changes to your lifestyle can help improve heart health. In some cases, changes to your diet may even reverse heart disease. If you have coronary artery disease (a condition where the blood vessels supplying your heart are narrowed or blocked), regular aerobic exercise can lower your risk of death. Talk to your doctor about safe ways to adjust your diet and exercise routine. They can help you create a plan that works for you.
Reference Simplification
Most people with coronary heart disease can feel healthy for years before they start experiencing symptoms.

Example 261 | Profile: older_adults | Language: English | ID/Index: 261
Source Text
The pain, lasting seconds to a few minutes, usually begins in the tonsillar region or at the base of the tongue and may radiate to the ipsilateral ear.
Generated Simplification
The pain usually starts in the area around the tonsils or at the base of the tongue. It lasts for a few seconds to a few minutes. The pain may also spread to the ear on the same side of the head.
Reference Simplification
The pain usually begins at the back of the tongue, back of the throat, or the area near the tonsils. Sometimes pain spreads to the ear or the area at the back of the jaw. The pain may last several seconds to a few minutes and usually affects only one side of the throat and tongue.

Source Text
In developing countries, most cases are probably caused by tuberculosis, particularly in patients with impaired immune function due to undernutrition or human immunodeficiency virus (HIV) infection.
Generated Simplification
In developing countries, tuberculosis is a common cause of this condition. It is more likely to affect people with weakened immune systems. This can happen due to: - Poor nutrition (undernutrition). - Infections like HIV (human immunodeficiency virus). If you think this might apply to you, talk to your doctor. They can help check for these conditions and recommend the right treatment.
Reference Simplification

Example 270 | Profile: older_adults | Language: English | ID/Index: 270
Source Text
Infection ranges from asymptomatic (in 5 to 50 % of cases) to a hemorrhagic fever with a case fatality rate of up to 50 %.
Generated Simplification
Infections can vary in how they affect people. - Some people may not have any symptoms at all. This happens in about 5% to 50% of cases. - In other cases, the infection can cause a serious illness called hemorrhagic fever. This condition can be life-threatening, with up to 50% of people not surviving. If you have concerns about symptoms or risks, talk to your healthcare provider.
Reference Simplification
Some infected people do not have symptoms. Others have mild symptoms, and some have a severe, life -threatening illness. Up to 50 % of people with severe bleeding and fever die.

Example 271 | Profile: older_adults | Language: English | ID/Index: 271
Source Text
Renovascular hypertension may develop if the abdominal aorta or renal arteries are narrowed.
Generated Simplification
Renovascular hypertension is a type of high blood pressure. It can happen if certain blood vessels become too narrow. These blood vessels include: - The abdominal aorta, which is a large artery in your belly. - The renal arteries, which are the blood vessels that carry blood to your kidneys. If you have concerns about high blood pressure or these blood vessels, talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
Kidneys: The kidneys may malfunction because the arteries that supply blood to the kidneys become narrowed. This narrowing can result in high blood pressure.

Example 272 | Profile: older_adults | Language: English | ID/Index: 272
Source Text
Unlike other dementias, multiple- infarct dementia tends to progress in discrete steps; each episode is accompanied by intellectual decline, sometimes followed by modest recovery.
Generated Simplification
### Understanding Multiple-Infarct Dementia - Multiple-infarct dementia is different from other types of dementia. - It usually gets worse in steps, not gradually. - Each step happens after an episode, such as a small stroke. - After an episode, a person’s thinking and memory may get worse. - Sometimes, there may be a small improvement after this decline. If you or a loved one notice sudden changes in memory or thinking, talk to a healthcare professional. They can help evaluate the situation and discuss next steps.
Reference Simplification
Unlike dementia caused by Alzheimer disease, vascular dementia may progress in steps. Symptoms may worsen suddenly, then plateau or lessen somewhat. They then become worse months or years later when another stroke occurs.

Example 273 | Profile: older_adults | Language: English | ID/Index: 273
Source Text
Intractable apneic spells require ventilator support.
Generated Simplification
Some breathing problems, called intractable apneic spells, can be very serious. These spells happen when a person stops breathing and cannot start again on their own. In these cases, a ventilator may be needed. A ventilator is a machine that helps a person breathe by moving air in and out of their lungs. If you or a loved one experiences severe breathing problems, it is important to seek medical care immediately. Talk to your healthcare provider if you have concerns about breathing issues or the need for ventilator support.
Reference Simplification
Newborns who have apnea spells that are difficult to treat may need a ventilator (a machine that helps air get in and out of the lungs) to help them breathe.

Example 274 | Profile: older_adults | Language: English | ID/Index: 274
Source Text
Diagnosis of DiGeorge syndrome is based on clinical findings. An absolute lymphocyte count is done, followed by B- and T- cell counts and evaluation of lymphocyte subsets if leukopenia is detected; blood tests to evaluate T- cell and parathyroid function are done. Ig levels and vaccine titers are measured. If complete DiGeorge syndrome is suspected, the T- cell receptor excision circle (TREC) test should also be done. A lateral chest x-ray may help evaluate thymic shadow. Fluorescent in situ hybridization (FISH) testing can detect the chromosomal deletion in the 22q11 region; standard chromosomal tests to check for other abnormalities may also be done.
Generated Simplification
### Diagnosing DiGeorge Syndrome Doctors use several tests to diagnose DiGeorge syndrome. These tests help check how your immune system and other body systems are working. Here’s what to expect: #### Initial Tests - **Blood Tests**: A blood test will measure the number of lymphocytes (a type of white blood cell). If the lymphocyte count is low, further tests will check B cells and T cells, which are important for your immune system. - **T-Cell and Parathyroid Function**: Additional blood tests will check how well your T cells and parathyroid glands are working. The parathyroid glands help control calcium levels in your body. #### Additional Tests - **Immunoglobulin (Ig) Levels and Vaccine Response**: Doctors will measure your Ig levels (proteins that fight infections) and check how your body responds to vaccines. - **TREC Test**: If a severe form of DiGeorge syndrome (called complete DiGeorge syndrome) is suspected, a special test called the TREC test may be done. This test looks for signs of T-cell development problems. #### Imaging - **Chest X-Ray**: A chest x-ray may be done to check for the thymus gland. The thymus is important for your immune system, and its size can sometimes be seen on the x-ray. #### Genetic Testing - **FISH Test**: This test looks for a specific missing piece of chromosome 22 (called the 22q11 deletion). This deletion is a common cause of DiGeorge syndrome. - **Other Chromosome Tests**: Standard genetic tests may also be done to check for other possible genetic changes. ### What This Means for You - These tests help doctors understand how DiGeorge syndrome is affecting your body. - If you have questions about these tests or what they involve, talk to your healthcare provider. - Some of these tests may require follow-up appointments or additional discussions with specialists. ### Next Steps - If you or your doctor suspect DiGeorge syndrome, ask about starting these tests. - Discuss any unusual symptoms or concerns with your healthcare provider. - Follow up on test results to understand what they mean for your health.
Reference Simplification
Sometimes imaging tests (such as chest x-rays and echocardiography). Doctors suspect DiGeorge syndrome based on symptoms. Blood tests are done for the following reasons: To determine the total number of blood cells and the number of T and B cells; To evaluate how well T cells and the parathyroid gland are functioning; To determine how well the body produces immunoglobulins in response to vaccines. A chest x-ray may be taken to check the size of the thymus gland.

Example 275 | Profile: older_adults | Language: English | ID/Index: 275
Source Text
It had a cranial capacity of 400-500 cc. In 2006, Lee Berger announced the Taung Child probably was killed by an eagle or other large predatory bird since the similarity of damage to the skull and eye sockets of the Taung Child is of that seen in modern primates that are known to have been killed by eagles.
Generated Simplification
The Taung Child had a brain size of about 400 to 500 cubic centimeters (cc). In 2006, a researcher named Lee Berger shared an important finding. He suggested that the Taung Child was likely killed by an eagle or a large bird of prey. This idea came from studying the damage to the skull and eye sockets. The injuries were similar to those seen in modern primates that have been attacked by eagles.
Reference Simplification
This conclusion was reached by noting similarities in the damage to the skull and eye sockets of the Taung Child to the skulls of primates known to have been killed by modern eagles. The left hand side of the endocast of the Taung child is covered in beautiful calcium carbonate crystals.

Example 276 | Profile: older_adults | Language: English | ID/Index: 276
Source Text
Renal involvement can develop at any time and may be the only manifestation of SLE. It may be benign and asymptomatic or progressive and fatal.
Generated Simplification
Kidney problems can happen at any time in people with lupus (SLE). - Sometimes, these kidney issues cause no symptoms and are not harmful. - In other cases, they can get worse over time and become life-threatening. If you have lupus, talk to your doctor about your risk for kidney problems and any symptoms to watch for.
Reference Simplification
Kidney involvement may be minor and without symptoms or may be progressive and fatal. The kidneys can be affected at any time and may be the only organ affected by lupus.

Example 278 | Profile: older_adults | Language: English | ID/Index: 278
Source Text
Vasoconstriction can change a lesion that does not limit blood flow into a severe or complete stenosis. Symptoms of unstable angina or myocardial infarction, ischemic stroke, or rest pain in the limbs may develop when unstable plaques rupture and acutely occlude a major artery, with superimposition of thrombosis or embolism.
Generated Simplification
When blood vessels tighten (a process called vasoconstriction), it can make a mild blockage in the blood flow much worse. In some cases, this can lead to a severe or complete blockage. If a plaque (a fatty deposit in the artery) becomes unstable and ruptures, it can suddenly block a major artery. This blockage may also involve a blood clot (thrombosis) or a piece of material traveling through the bloodstream (embolism). When this happens, it can cause serious problems, such as: - Chest pain or discomfort from unstable angina or a heart attack. - A stroke caused by reduced blood flow to the brain. - Severe pain in the arms or legs when at rest, due to poor blood flow. If you experience any of these symptoms, seek medical help immediately. Talk to your doctor if you have concerns about your heart or blood vessels. They can help assess your risk and recommend steps to protect your health.
Reference Simplification
The first symptom of a narrowed artery may be pain or cramps at times when blood flow can not keep up with the tissues â need for oxygen.

Example 279 | Profile: older_adults | Language: English | ID/Index: 279
Source Text
According to the fifth Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM), DID includes `` the presence of two or more distinct identities or personality states '' that alternate control of the individual 's behavior, accompanied by the inability to recall personal information beyond what is expected through normal forgetfulness.
Generated Simplification
Dissociative Identity Disorder (DID) - DID is a mental health condition. - A person with DID has two or more distinct identities or personality states. - These identities take turns controlling the person's behavior. - People with DID often cannot remember personal information. This memory loss is more severe than normal forgetfulness. If you think this might apply to you or someone you know, talk to a healthcare professional for guidance.
Reference Simplification
According to the DSM-IV, the symptoms of DID are `` the presence of two or more distinct identities or personality states '' (which are often called `` alters ''), as well as forgetting things that people normally do n't forget.

Source Text
Scurvy or subclinical scurvy is caused by the lack of vitamin C. In modern Western societies, scurvy is rarely present in adults, although infants and elderly people are affected.

Scurvy is a condition caused by not getting enough vitamin C. In modern Western countries, scurvy is uncommon in adults. However, it can still affect certain groups, such as infants and older adults. If you are concerned about your vitamin C levels or symptoms of scurvy, talk to your healthcare provider.

Example 298 | Profile: older_adults | Language: English | ID/Index: 298
Source Text
Arterial blood carries oxygen from inhaled air to all of the cells of the body, and venous blood carries carbon dioxide, a waste product of metabolism by cells, to the lungs to be exhaled.
Generated Simplification
Arterial blood is the blood that carries oxygen from the air you breathe to all the cells in your body. Venous blood is the blood that carries carbon dioxide, which is a waste product made by your cells, back to your lungs. Your lungs then remove this carbon dioxide when you exhale.
Reference Simplification
Red blood cells carry oxygen and carbon dioxide around our body.

Example 299 | Profile: older_adults | Language: English | ID/Index: 299
Source Text
The kinetoplastids (also known as ` kinetoplastea ') are a group of single-cell flagellate protozoa, including a number of parasites responsible for serious diseases in humans and other animals, as well as various forms found in soil and aquatic environments.
Generated Simplification
Kinetoplastids are tiny, single-celled organisms. They have a tail-like structure called a flagellum that helps them move. Some kinetoplastids are parasites. This means they can live inside humans or animals and cause serious diseases. Other types of kinetoplastids are harmless and can be found in soil or water. If you have questions about diseases caused by these parasites, talk to your doctor.
Reference Simplification
The kinetoplastids are a group of single-cell flagellate Eukaryotes.
